name: Modular Implicits
summary: White, Bour, and Yallop's research proposal (ML Workshop 2014) extending OCaml with implicit functor application, similar to how Haskell elaborates type-class instances; demonstrates that type classes and ML modules are duals — type classes are dictionaries-passed-implicitly, ML modules are dictionaries-passed-explicitly.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: White, Bour, and Yallop's **Modular Implicits** (ML Workshop 2014) propose extending OCaml with a mechanism for *implicitly* applying functors to their argument structures, similar to how Haskell elaborates type-class instances. The user writes `show 42` and the elaborator searches the visible scope for a structure matching `SHOW with type t = int`, applying the appropriate functor automatically.

sections.title: Duality with type classes
sections.content:
This is conceptually significant because it shows that **type classes and module systems are duals** in a precise sense. Type classes are dictionaries-passed-implicitly; ML modules are dictionaries-passed-explicitly. Modular implicits make ML modules participate in implicit elaboration, recovering type-class ergonomics without abandoning the module-calculus foundation.

Scala's implicits (Scala 2) and `given`/`using` (Scala 3) are independent designs in the same conceptual space; Rust's traits are another point. The four — Haskell type classes, Scala implicits, Rust traits, and modular implicits — span the design space of "how does the language handle the dictionary-passing problem?"

sections.title: Non-adoption and design space
sections.content:
The proposal has not landed in mainline OCaml, partly because the elaboration semantics interact subtly with OCaml's existing type inference and module language. The design space remains open: OCaml's existing first-class modules cover some of the use cases, and the community has debated whether implicit modules add enough value to justify the language complexity.

The lesson generalises: **adding implicit elaboration to an existing module system is feasible but expensive**. Languages designing from scratch should pick one of (a) implicit type classes Haskell-style, (b) explicit functors ML-style, (c) implicit functors Scala-style, or (d) traits Rust-style — and commit; mixing them tends to produce confusing elaboration semantics.
